1: Awake, awake, put on your strength, O Zion — put on your beautiful garments, O Jerusalem the holy city. For there shall no more come into you the uncircumcised and the unclean. The double awake, awake mirrors Isaiah 51:9 (arm of the LORD) and Isaiah 51:17 (Jerusalem) — the third and climactic summons. The beautiful garments replace the exile's rags and the mourning dress of Babylon. Purity categories (uncircumcised, unclean) defined sacred space access throughout the ANE; the restored holy city will enforce covenant boundaries. Revelation 21:27 echoes this exclusion.
2: Shake yourself from the dust and arise — be seated, O Jerusalem. Loose the bonds from your neck, O captive daughter of Zion. Dust-sitting was the posture of public mourning in the ANE; rising from the dust is the gesture of restoration to dignity. The neck-bond is the literal yoke of servitude — a common iconographic symbol in Assyrian and Babylonian relief art showing captive peoples with rope-collars.
3: For thus says YHWH: You were sold for nothing and you shall be redeemed without money (chinnam). The technical term chinnam (for nothing, gratis) appears in both the selling and the redemption: neither transaction involved payment. Israel's exile was not a legitimate commercial transaction but an act of divine discipline; the return requires no ransom price, because YHWH owns the outcome.
4: For thus says the Lord YHWH: My people went down at the first into Egypt to sojourn there and the Assyrian oppressed them for nothing. The historical survey begins with Egypt (the first sojourn-and-oppression cycle) and adds Assyria (the second). The pattern of oppression-for-nothing recurs across Israel's history — each oppressor acted without legitimate claim.
5: Now therefore what have I here — declares YHWH — for my people are taken away for nothing? Their rulers wail, declares YHWH, and continually all the day my name is despised. YHWH's name being despised by the nations because of Israel's exile is the Ezekiel 36:20–22 logic precisely: the nations said, These are YHWH's people and yet they had to go out of his land — making YHWH appear impotent. Israel's restoration is motivated in part by divine reputation.
6: Therefore my people shall know my name — therefore in that day they shall know that it is I who speak; here am I. The repeated therefore drives the logic: name-despising → name-vindication → covenant knowledge. The self-presentation formula here am I (hineni) is the divine answer to a human call — YHWH responds to his own summons.
7: How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news (mevasser) — who publishes peace, who brings good news of happiness, who publishes salvation, who says to Zion, Your God reigns! The herald-feet image captures the desperate waiting of a city for battle news. Mevasser is the verbal root behind Greek euangelion (gospel). Your God reigns is the royal acclamation — the new king's enthronement announcement. Paul quotes this verse (Romans 10:15); Mark 1:15 (the kingdom of God is at hand) is its fulfillment announcement.
8: The voice of your watchmen — they lift up their voice, together they sing for joy. For eye to eye they see the return of YHWH to Zion. Watchmen on city walls were the first to see approaching messengers. Here all the watchmen simultaneously see YHWH's return — the divine warrior coming back to his city after the exile judgment. Eye-to-eye seeing is unmediated, certain perception — no ambiguity, no prophetic vision, direct sight.
9: Break forth together into singing, you waste places of Jerusalem — for YHWH has comforted his people; he has redeemed Jerusalem. The ruined places of the city become the singing choir. The comfort (nicham) and redemption (ga'al) announce that both the pastoral and the legal-kinsman dimensions of YHWH's action are complete.
10: YHWH has bared his holy arm before the eyes of all the nations and all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God. Baring the arm is the warrior's preparation-for-combat gesture — rolling up the sleeve to strike. The holy arm is visible to all nations simultaneously: the new Exodus is a universal, public event, not a private tribal transaction.
11: Depart, depart, go out from there — touch no unclean thing. Go out from the midst of her and be pure, you who bear the vessels of YHWH. The purity-departure call applies specifically to the temple-vessel carriers — the Levitical personnel responsible for the sacred equipment Nebuchadnezzar took to Babylon (2 Kings 25:13–17). The return of the vessels (fulfilled under Cyrus, Ezra 1:7–11) is a sacral procession requiring ritual purity. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Corinthians 6:17 as the basis for the community's separation from idolatry.
12: For you shall not go out in haste and you shall not go in flight — for YHWH will go before you and the God of Israel will be your rear guard. The deliberate, unhurried nature of the new Exodus contrasts with the Passover night exodus from Egypt (be-chipazon — in haste, Exodus 12:11; Deuteronomy 16:3). YHWH as vanguard and rear guard forms the divine sandwich that protects the march — both military and liturgical imagery.
13: Behold, my servant shall act wisely — he shall be high and lifted up and shall be exalted. The Fourth Servant Song opens (Isaiah 52:13–Isaiah 53:12). Three ascending Hebrew verbs: yaskil (prosper/act wisely), yarum (be high), nissa (be lifted up), gav'ah (be exalted very high). Isaiah 6:1 uses the same verb pair (ram ve-nissa) to describe YHWH on his throne — the Servant shares the divine elevation vocabulary.
14: As many were appalled at him — his appearance was so marred, beyond human semblance, and his form beyond that of the children of mankind. The appalled response (shamemu) is the reaction to something so horrible it silences observers. The marring language is extreme: the Servant's disfigurement exceeds the human form entirely — a body destroyed beyond recognition. This is read in Christian tradition as the crucifixion's physical reality.
15: So he shall startle many nations — kings shall shut their mouths because of him. For that which has not been told them they see and that which they have not heard they understand. The verb yazzeh (startle/sprinkle) is debated: it can mean to cause nations to leap/start in shock, or it can be the priestly sprinkling term used in ritual purification (Leviticus 4:6; Isaiah 16:14–19). If the latter, the Servant performs a cosmic purification of many nations — a priestly act on an international scale. Kings' silenced mouths is the courtroom gesture of speechlessness before an overwhelming divine revelation.
